title: Most odd degree hyperelliptic curves have only one rational point
speaker: Bjorn Poonen
abstract: We prove that the probability that a curve of the form $y^2 = f(x)$ over $\mathbb Q$ with $\deg f = 2g + 1$ has no rational point other than the point at infinity tends to 1 as $g$ tends to infinity. This is joint work with Michael Stoll.

proof_stages.label: Odd-degree hyperelliptic family and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes monic separable odd-degree hyperelliptic curves y^2=f(x) of degree 2g+1, notes their canonical rational point at infinity, and states that for g at least 3 a positive fraction—and asymptotically almost all as g grows—have no other rational points, effectively by 2-adic Chabauty.

proof_stages.label: Chabauty intersection and logarithmic directions
proof_stages.summary: Embedding C into its Jacobian using the point at infinity, the speaker reviews Chabauty's method as an intersection between C(Q_p) and the p-adic closure of J(Q), then uses the p-adic logarithm and reduction to projective F_p-directions to reduce the problem to separating two images modulo p, with torsion requiring separate treatment.

proof_stages.label: Selmer groups as global control of Mordell-Weil directions
proof_stages.summary: The Mordell-Weil group is bounded and located via the p-Selmer group, and the key Bhargava-Gross input is that the average size of the 2-Selmer group in congruence-defined families is 3 and its nonzero elements become equidistributed under the relevant local map.

proof_stages.label: Diagrammatic criterion plus torsion exclusion
proof_stages.summary: A lemma shows that injectivity of the local Selmer map and disjointness between the projectivized Selmer image and the projectivized curve image force rational points to lie in odd torsion, while a generic Manin-Mumford-type lemma excludes such torsion intersections for 100% of curves except the expected Weierstrass two-torsion.

proof_stages.label: Probabilistic reduction to a small local curve image
proof_stages.summary: Using equidistribution and the average size 3 of 2-Selmer groups, the proof reduces to showing that the average size of the projectivized 2-adic logarithmic image of C(Q_2) is tiny compared with 2^g, concretely bounded by O(g), so random Selmer elements miss it with high probability.

proof_stages.label: Residue-disk and polynomial-map bound for the local image
proof_stages.summary: For each 2-adic residue disk, the logarithm is expressed by integrating power series coming from holomorphic differentials, then replaced up to 2-adic units by bounded-degree polynomials, giving a lemma bounding the projective image of a disk by a linear function of the degree.

proof_stages.label: Bad reduction average via regular models
proof_stages.summary: To handle the arbitrarily bad 2-adic reduction occurring in a density-one set, the proof estimates the average number of smooth F_2-points on the minimal proper regular model and shows it is less than 3, which combined with the per-disk bound yields the required O(g) average local image bound and completes the theorem.

examples.name: Even-degree hyperelliptic curves with no rational points
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the odd-degree theorem with the known even-degree case, where most curves have no rational points and the proof uses local obstructions on double covers rather than Chabauty.
examples.engagement_reason: The even-degree case is cited as a contrasting theorem with a brief description of its methods, but no argument is carried out on such a curve.

examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws and reasons through the rank-one closure inside a two-dimensional p-adic Jacobian, explaining how multiples line up into a one-dimensional analytic subvariety and intersect the curve.

examples.name: Good reduction at 2 warm-up
examples.note: The good-reduction case illustrates the residue-disk strategy, since the number of disks is just the number of F_2-points and is bounded by the hyperelliptic double cover of P^1.
examples.engagement_reason: In the good-reduction-at-2 warm-up, the speaker decomposes C(Q2) into residue disks, counts the F2-points via the hyperelliptic map, and analyzes the log image on each disk.
